title: Standing-privilege credential vaulting drift — admin accounts outside PAM
exclusions: Does NOT evaluate usage of credentials (covered by 22.40.1); focuses on credentials sitting outside the vault entirely.
evidence: Saved search 'pam_vaulting_drift' (daily) cross-references IdP privileged groups against PAM vault inventory and reports accounts not present in the vault.

compliance.assurance_rationale: AC-5 separation of duties and privileged-account vaulting are directly enforced.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST-800-53 AC-5 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.40.4: Standing-privilege credential vaulting drift — admin accounts outside PAM.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.40.4' running on IdP privileged-group membership (Azure AD, Okta, LDAP), PAM vault inventory (CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Delinea)., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI 7.2.5.1 requires tight controls on privileged account lifecycle; unvaulted admins fail this control.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PCI-DSS 7.2.5.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.40.4: Standing-privilege credential vaulting drift — admin accounts outside PAM.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.40.4' running on IdP privileged-group membership (Azure AD, Okta, LDAP), PAM vault inventory (CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Delinea)., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Unvaulted privileged credentials are a textbook SOX-ITGC material weakness.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SOX-ITGC ITGC.AccessMgmt.Privileged (Privileged access) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.40.4: Standing-privilege credential vaulting drift — admin accounts outside PAM.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.40.4' running on IdP privileged-group membership (Azure AD, Okta, LDAP), PAM vault inventory (CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Delinea)., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: A domain-admin account exists in AzureAD but has no matching record in the CyberArk vault; the UC fires the next day.
controlTest.negativeScenario: All IdP admin accounts are present in the vault; no alert.

dataSources: IdP privileged-group membership (Azure AD, Okta, LDAP), PAM vault inventory (CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Delinea).
spl:
| rest /services/identity/privileged-groups splunk_server=*
| fields user group
| join type=outer user [| rest /services/pam/vault-inventory splunk_server=* | fields user vault_id]
| where isnull(vault_id)
| stats values(group) as groups by user
| table user groups
description: Treats the PAM vault as the authoritative register of privileged credentials and alerts on any IdP-level admin that is not present in the vault. The asymmetry between 'privileged' and 'managed' is the most common drift pattern.
value: Replaces the quarterly 'access-review export + spreadsheet join' with a daily evidence feed.
implementation: (1) Expose IdP privileged groups and PAM vault inventory via REST; (2) run daily; (3) on miss, auto-open a PAM onboarding ticket with the CISO.
visualization: Table of drift accounts by group, bar chart of drift count by tenant, single-value '% of admins vaulted'. Key fields: `groups`, `user`.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK — T1078.003 Valid Accounts: Local Accounts

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `AC-5`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIST-800-53 AC-5 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.40.4: Standing-privilege credential vaulting drift — admin accounts outside PAM." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Expose IdP privileged groups and PAM vault inventory via REST. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** run daily. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** on miss, auto-open a PAM onboarding ticket with the CISO. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Standing-privilege credential vaulting drift — admin accounts outside PAM," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the NIST-800-53 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`the outer`, sourcetype=`the primary`) with a subsearch on `user` across the full search range. `type=outer` (left-outer) returns every row from the outer search and fills nulls where the inner search has no match. Use this when the compliance question is 'for every row in A, what does B say?' — the null pattern is itself the finding (e.g. 'ticket opened but no PIR recorded').

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.40.4: Standing-privilege credential vaulting drift — admin account]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.40.4,reg=NIST-800-53
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `user`, `group`, `vault_id`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table user, group, vault_id
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.40.4: NIST-800-53 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Drift Accounts By Group (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Bar Chart Of Drift Count By Tenant (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value '% Of Admins Vaulted' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIST-800-53-specific failure modes:**

• Control baseline selection: NIST 800-53 has Low/Moderate/High/Privacy baselines via FIPS 199 categorisation. A detection written to a High baseline threshold produces false alarms against Low-categorised systems. Join with the system categorisation register `nist_800_53_system_categorization.csv` before thresholding.

• CA-7 continuous monitoring cadence: specifies 'ongoing' authorisation activities. Detections supporting CA-7 must produce metrics at the organisation's ConMon cadence (monthly is common for most agencies) — weekly fires are fine but not themselves evidence of monthly compliance.

• Control inheritance: in a cloud-hybrid deployment, some controls are inherited from the Cloud Service Provider. Detections must not flag 'missing' controls the CSP provides (documented in the SSP via Control Inheritance matrix); reference the SSP appendix and suppress matching findings.

• SP 800-53 Rev 5 restructure: controls were renumbered between Rev 4 and Rev 5. Detections that cite Rev 4 numbering (e.g. AC-2.12) may not match the SSP if the organisation has migrated. Store both Rev numbers in the UC metadata and reconcile.
grandmaExplanation: We help you show who used powerful accounts and when, so access reviews and investigations have a clear story.
